# Cryo-EM structures of human organic anion transporting polypeptide OATP1B1

## Metadata
**Authors:** Ziyang Shan, Xuemei Yang, Huihui Liu, Yafei Yuan, Yuan Xiao, Jing Nan, Wei Zhang, Wenqi Song, Jufang Wang, Feiwen Wei, Yanqing Zhang
**Journal:** Cell Research
**Date:** 2023 Sep 6
**DOI:** [10.1038/s41422-023-00870-8](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41422-023-00870-8)
**PMID:** 37674011
**PMCID:** PMC10709409
**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC10709409/
**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10709409/pdf/41422_2023_Article_870.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10709409/pdf/41422_2023_Article_870.pdf)

## Abstract

Members of the solute carrier organic anion transporting polypeptide (OATPs) family function as transporters for a large variety of amphipathic organic anions including endogenous metabolites and clinical drugs, such as bile salts, steroids, thyroid hormones, statins, antibiotics, antivirals, and anticancer drugs. OATP1B1 plays a vital role in transporting such substances into the liver for hepatic clearance. FDA and EMA recommend conducting in vitro testing of drug‚Äìdrug interactions (DDIs) involving OATP1B1. However, the structure and working mechanism of OATPs still remains elusive. In this study, we determined cryo-EM structures of human OATP1B1 bound with representative endogenous metabolites (bilirubin and estrone-3-sulfate), a clinical drug (simeprevir), and a fluorescent indicator (2‚Ä≤,7‚Ä≤-dichlorofluorescein), in both outward- and inward-open states. These structures reveal major and minor substrate binding pockets and conformational changes during transport. In combination with mutagenesis studies and molecular dynamics simulations, our work comprehensively elucidates the transport mechanism of OATP1B1 and provides the structural basis for DDI predictions involving OATP1B1, which will greatly promote our understanding of OATPs.

Subject terms: Cryoelectron microscopy, Transport carrier

## Introduction

Encoded by genes of *SLCO*^1^ solute carrier family (formerly called *SLC21*), the members of the organic anion transporting polypeptides (OATPs) family transport a wide range of amphipathic organic anions across the plasma membrane, including endogenous and exogenous substances, such as bile salts, steroids, thyroid hormones, and clinical drugs.^1,2^ Eleven members of human OATPs have been identified and categorized into 6 subfamilies based on their amino acid composition, namely OATP1‚Äì6 (Supplementary information, Fig. [S1](#MOESM13)). Among these OATPs, each member exhibits distinct but partially overlapping substrate specificity, which varies according to their specific tissue distribution.^3^ OATP1B1, encoded by *SLCO1B1* and formerly known as SLC21A6, LST-1, OATP-C, or OATP2, is specifically expressed on the basolateral membrane of hepatocytes.^4^ OATP1B1 plays a vital role in mediating the uptake of endogenous compounds such as bile acids, bilirubin, steroid conjugates, and thyroid hormones, as well as a large variety of clinical drugs including statins, antibiotics, antivirals, and anticancer medications for hepatic clearance (Fig. [1a](#Fig1)).^5‚Äì7^ Dysfunction of OATP1B1 can lead to statin-induced myopathy^8^ and hyperbilirubinemia,^9^ as exemplified by Rotor syndrome, a type of hereditary hyperbilirubinemia.^10^

### Fig. 1. Structure determination and overall structures of human OATP1B1 in the presence of substrates and inhibitor.

![Fig. 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/d665/10709409/fe1a68786b2b/41422_2023_870_Fig1_HTML.jpg)

[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=10709409_41422_2023_870_Fig1_HTML.jpg)

a Schematic representation of the biological functions of OATP1B1. b Chemical structures of the four compounds used in this study. c The topology of OATP1B1. NTD and CTD are colored in gold and marine, respectively. EL3‚Äì4, EL5‚Äì6 and EL9‚Äì10 are colored in green, cyan and purple, respectively. Dashed lines show the flexible regions in our structures. d, e Side views of cryo-EM reconstructions (d) and cartoon representations (e) of human OATP1B1 in apo state and bound with bilirubin, E3S, simeprevir and DCF, colored in cyan, green, magenta, and yellow, respectively. OATP1B1 is shown with NTD and CTD colored in gold and marine, respectively.

Numerous studies have emphasized the central role of OATP1B1 in drug‚Äìdrug interactions (DDIs).^5,11‚Äì13^ Food and Drug Administration and European Medicines Agency recommend conducting in vitro testing to evaluate potential OATP1B1 interactions.^14,15^ Many drugs with significant clinical implications act as substrates and/or inhibitors of OATP1B1.^5^ For instance, in clinical practice, combination therapy involving lipid-lowering statins^16^ (e.g., atorvastatin) and various drugs, such as antihypertensive medications, antibiotics, and antiviral agents, is commonly employed. However, certain medications, such as gemfibrozil, cyclosporine A, rifampicin, and ritonavir, can suppress the function of OATP1B1, leading to excessive systemic exposure of the affected drug, including statins.^11^ The elevation of statin blood levels can potentially lead to fatal myopathy and rhabdomyolysis.^17,18^

The energy coupling transport mechanism of OATPs is still not fully understood. The transport of OATPs is independent of Na^+^, K^+^, Cl^‚àí^ gradients, membrane potential and ATP levels.^19‚Äì21^ OATPs are generally believed to act as organic anion exchangers, coupled with the efflux of anions, such as HCO_3_^‚àí^ and glutathione (GSH), but direct evidence was only provided in rat Oatp1a1.^22,23^ However, transport of OATP1B1/OATP1B3 was most probably demonstrated to occur through bidirectional facilitated diffusion, which was unaffected by GSH.^21^ Moreover, evidence has been presented for pH-dependence in transport stimulation for most OATPs with the exception of OATP1C1.^24,25^

To elucidate the transport mechanism of OATP1B1, we determined the structures of OATP1B1 bound with representative endogenous substrates (bilirubin and estrone-3-sulfate (E3S)), a clinical drug (simeprevir), as well as a fluorescent indicator (2‚Ä≤,7‚Ä≤-dichlorofluorescein (DCF)). These structures reveal unexpected major and minor substrate binding pockets and conformational changes between outward-open and inward-open states. Together with mutagenesis functional studies and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, our work elucidates the transport mechanism of OATP1B1, and provides the structural basis for DDI predictions involving OATP1B1, which will promote the understanding of OATPs.

## Results

### Structure determination

Human OATP1B1 was expressed in Expi293F cells and purified using tandem affinity and size exclusion chromatography. To investigate the transporting mechanism of OATP1B1, we supplemented the protein solution with a saturating concentration of endogenous substrates, bilirubin and E3S, and a clinical drug, simeprevir, during cryo-sample preparation. To better explain the binding mode of the fluorescent probe, DCF, which is employed in the following transport assays, we also prepared the cryo-sample of OATP1B1‚ÄìDCF (Fig. [1b](#Fig1)). The purified OATP1B1 protein alone or supplemented with ligands was then subjected to cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) single particle analysis. For simplicity, OATP1B1 bound with each ligand is hereafter referred to OATP1B1‚ÄìB (bilirubin), OATP1B1‚ÄìE (E3S), OATP1B1‚ÄìS (simeprevir) and OATP1B1‚ÄìD (DCF).

We determined the structures of OATP1B1-apo/B/S/D in outward-open state at resolutions ranging from 2.9 √Ö to 3.7 √Ö (Fig. [1d](#Fig1)). The particle dataset of OATP1B1‚ÄìE contained a mixture of outward-open, inward-open, and occluded states, namely OATP1B1-E_ou_, OATP1B1‚ÄìE_in_ and OATP1B1‚ÄìE_oc_, respectively. After extensive trials, we successfully obtained the inward-open structure of OATP1B1‚ÄìE_in_ at a resolution of 3.2 √Ö (Fig. [1d](#Fig1); Supplementary information, Fig [S5](#MOESM17)), with OATP1B1‚ÄìE_ou_ and OATP1B1‚ÄìE_oc_ resolved at moderate to low resolutions. For details of cryo-EM data acquisition and processing, please refer to Materials and methods (Supplementary information, Figs. [S2](#MOESM14)‚Äì[S6](#MOESM18) and Table [S1](#MOESM6)). The ligand model building was based on the EM densities in a combination of ligand-free docking and MD simulations (Supplementary information, Fig. [S7](#MOESM19) and Table [S2](#MOESM7)). The relatively small and rigid substances, E3S and DCF, revealed stable binding with OATP1B1, while larger ligands, bilirubin and simeprevir, exhibited more flexibility during MD simulations (Supplementary information, Videos [S1](#MOESM1)‚Äì[S4](#MOESM4)).

### Overall architecture

The overall architecture of OATP1B1 is composed of the transmembrane domain (TMD) and extracellular loops (ELs) with both N- and C-terminal ends situated in the cytoplasm. The TMD follows the canonical MFS fold^26^ and consists of 12 transmembrane helices (TMs) organized into an N-terminal domain (NTD) and a C-terminal domain (CTD), exhibiting a 2-fold pseudo symmetry around an axis perpendicular to the membrane plane (Fig. [1c](#Fig1)). The NTD and CTD are connected by an intracellular helix (ICH) (Fig. [2a, b](#Fig2)).

### Fig. 2. Overall architecture and central cavity environment of OATP1B1.

![Fig. 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/d665/10709409/bde2442d065f/41422_2023_870_Fig2_HTML.jpg)

[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=10709409_41422_2023_870_Fig2_HTML.jpg)

a Overall architecture of outward-open OATP1B1-apo. NTD and CTD are colored in gold and marine, respectively. b Overall architecture of inward-open OATP1B1‚ÄìEin. c Multiple disulfide bonds formed by conserved cysteines within EL9‚Äì10 are shown as yellow sticks. A fragment of EL3‚Äì4 termed as N-lariat, is attached to EL9‚Äì10 via a disulfide bond. d Electropositive residues along the translocation path of outward-open OATP1B1-apo. e Slab view showing the conservation level of OATP1B1-apo using ConSurf. f Sliced view of the surface electrostatic potential of OATP1B1-apo. g Top view of key residues in the central cavity of outward-open OATP1B1-apo, forming electropositive, electronegative, and hydrophobic parts. Residues are shown as stick and colored based on ConSurf analysis.

The largest extracellular loop, EL9‚Äì10, features several Œ≤ sheets and pairs of disulfide bonds, known as the Kazal domain^27^ (Fig. [2c](#Fig2)). The Kazal domain is an evolutionarily conserved protein domain, usually indicative of serine protease inhibitors,^28^ but its function in OATPs still remains elusive. Within EL9‚Äì10, two glycosylation sites (N503 and N516) were observed to be covalently linked to N-acetylglucosamine sugar moieties (Fig. [2a, b](#Fig2)). In both outward- and inward-open states of OATP1B1, EL3‚Äì4 associates with the Kazal domain through a conserved disulfide bond C142‚ÄìC463, designated as N-lariat (Fig. [2c](#Fig2)). MD simulations of the outward-open states with EL3‚Äì4 disconnected resulted in abnormally wider opening (data not shown), indicating that the N-lariat potentially regulates the maximum opening extent and acts as an extracellular halter connecting the extracellular halves.

### Substrate entrance and central cavity environment

The translocation path is lined with multiple basic residues (Fig. [2d](#Fig2)). An extracellular electropositive opening, enclosed by EL5‚Äì6, the Kazal domain and N-terminus of TM8, potentially functions as an entrance for organic anion substrates to access the central cavity (Supplementary information, Fig. [S8a, b](#MOESM20)). Most of the positively-charged residues are distributed at this indicated entrance rather than in the cavity itself, implying that selectivity for organic anions might be achieved by partially filtering out cations at the cavity entrance. Besides, in the outward-open OATP1B1-apo/B/S/D, lipid-like EM densities were trapped in the V-shaped lateral opening formed by the two halves between TM2 and TM11 (Supplementary information, Fig. [S8c](#MOESM20)), which might serve as another potential access for hydrophobic substrates, as described in OCT3.^29^

The interface between NTD and CTD of OATP1B1 forms a conserved cavity in which substrates bind (Fig. [2e](#Fig2)). The central cavity exhibits evident features of both hydrophobicity and electro-positivity, wherein TMs 5, 7, 8, and 10 contribute to the hydrophobic side while TMs 1, 2, 4, and 11 form the charged side partly localized oppositely to the hydrophobic side (Figs. [2f, g](#Fig2), and [3c](#Fig3)). Positively-charged residues, K41/K49/R580, along with modestly conserved aromatic residues, i.e., Y352, F356, and F386, create an ideal environment for selecting and translocating amphipathic organic anion substrates within the central cavity. Of note, all three basic residues participate in generating charge pairs, K41‚ÄìE185, K49‚ÄìD70 and highly conserved R580‚ÄìE74 (Figs. [2e, g](#Fig2) and [3c, d](#Fig3)).

### Fig. 3. Substrate binding sites in the outward-open state.

![Fig. 3](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/d665/10709409/d82359cbb47d/41422_2023_870_Fig3_HTML.jpg)

[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=10709409_41422_2023_870_Fig3_HTML.jpg)

a Superimposition of outward-open structures of OATP1B1-apo/B/S/D. b Bilirubin and simeprevir occupy the major pocket and DCF occupies the minor pocket, which is superimposed and presented in the central cavity of OATP1B1‚ÄìD. c Top view of outward-open OATP1B1-apo showing key residues in the major pocket and minor pocket. Electropositive residues, electronegative residues, hydrophobic residues, and hydrophilic residues are colored in magenta, yellow, gray and cyan, respectively. d Sequence alignment of key residues in the central cavity of OATP1B1 among SLCO family transporters. e‚Äìg Local ligand density representations and extracellular views for substrate binding sites of OATP1B1‚ÄìB (e), OATP1B1‚ÄìS (f) and OATP1B1‚ÄìD (g) in outward-open state.

### Substrate binding sites in outward-open state

Bilirubin is a representative endogenous substrate of OATP1B1, which is a kind of biologically toxic metabolite produced as a byproduct of heme degradation in red blood cells. Impaired elimination of bilirubin can lead to hyperbilirubinemia, which is closely associated with multiple disorders and reflects abnormal liver function.^30^ As for simeprevir, a direct-acting antiviral drug for the treatment of hepatitis C virus infection, it not only acts as a substrate for OATP1B1 but also functions as an inhibitor.^31,32^ Simeprevir can lead to clinical DDIs, as exemplified by an approximately 2-fold increase in plasma when administered concomitantly with atorvastatin or rosuvastatin.^32^ DCF is a fluorescein derivative and has been previously identified to be a good substrate and probe to assess the transport activity of OATP1B1.^33‚Äì37^

Structures of OATP1B1 bound with bilirubin, simeprevir and DCF, adopt similar outward-open conformations as that of OATP1B1-apo (Fig. [3a](#Fig3)). The root mean square deviation between OATP1B1-apo and each of the ligand-bound states is 0.51 √Ö (apo vs bilirubin), 0.46 √Ö (apo vs simeprevir), and 0.89 √Ö (apo vs DCF), respectively. Typically, MFS transporters engage TMs 1, 4, 7, 10 (known as A helices) to bind ligands.^26,38^ However, OATP1B1 also employs B helices to bind substances, i.e., with TMs 5, 7, 8, and 11 to bind with bilirubin, TMs 1, 5, 7, 8, and 11 to bind with simeprevir, and TMs 7, 8, and 10 to bind with DCF.

Based on the ligand binding sites, two distinct pockets were observed within the translocation pathway. Bilirubin and simeprevir occupy the major pocket located in the central cavity, while DCF binds to the minor pocket extending into the NTD (Fig. [3b](#Fig3)). The binding of these ligands primarily relies on interactions with hydrophobic residues opposite to the charged side in the central cavity (Fig. [3c, e‚Äìg](#Fig3); Supplementary information, Fig. [S7](#MOESM19)). B/S/D are all closely attached to F356 and Y352 around the middle of TM7, further clamped by F386 from TM8, acting as a core binding hub. The EM densities of ligands of B/S/D were observed to clearly adhere to F356 (Fig. [3e‚Äìg](#Fig3)). However, no obviously direct associations were observed between ligands and the positively-charged residues in the central cavity.

1‚Üí# Identification of Glycochenodeoxycholate 3‚ÄêO‚ÄêGlucuronide and Glycodeoxycholate 3‚ÄêO‚ÄêGlucuronide as Highly Sensitive and Specific OATP1B1 Biomarkers
     2‚Üí
     3‚Üí## Metadata
     4‚Üí**Authors:** Mikko Neuvonen, P√§ivi Hirvensalo, Aleksi Tornio, Brian Rago, Mark West, Sarah Lazzaro, Sumathy Mathialagan, Manthena Varma, Matthew A Cerny, Chester Costales, Ragu Ramanathan, A David Rodrigues, Mikko Niemi
     5‚Üí**Journal:** Clinical Pharmacology and Therapeutics
     6‚Üí**Date:** 2020 Oct 18
     7‚Üí**DOI:** [10.1002/cpt.2053](https://doi.org/10.1002/cpt.2053)
     8‚Üí**PMID:** 32961594
     9‚Üí**PMCID:** PMC7983942
    10‚Üí**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7983942/
    11‚Üí**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7983942/pdf/CPT-109-646.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7983942/pdf/CPT-109-646.pdf)
    12‚Üí
    13‚Üí## Abstract
    14‚Üí
    15‚ÜíThe aim of this study was to investigate the sensitivity and specificity of endogenous glycochenodeoxycholate and glycodeoxycholate 3‚ÄêO‚Äêglucuronides (GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G) as substrates for organic anion transporting polypeptide 1B1 (OATP1B1) in humans. We measured fasting levels of plasma GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G using liquid chromatography‚Äêtandem mass spectrometry in 356 healthy volunteers. The mean plasma levels of both compounds were ~ 50% lower in women than in men (P = 2.25 √ó 10‚àí18 and P = 4.73 √ó 10‚àí9). In a microarray‚Äêbased genome‚Äêwide association study, the SLCO1B1 rs4149056 (c.521T>C, p.Val174Ala) variation showed the strongest association with the plasma GCDCA‚Äê3G (P = 3.09 √ó 10‚àí30) and GDCA‚Äê3G (P = 1.60 √ó 10‚àí17) concentrations. The mean plasma concentration of GCDCA‚Äê3G was 9.2‚Äêfold (P = 8.77 √ó 10‚àí31) and that of GDCA‚Äê3G was 6.4‚Äêfold (P = 2.45x10‚àí13) higher in individuals with the SLCO1B1 c.521C/C genotype than in those with the c.521T/T genotype. No other variants showed independent genome‚Äêwide significant associations with GCDCA‚Äê3G or GDCA‚Äê3G. GCDCA‚Äê3G was highly efficacious in detecting the SLCO1B1 c.521C/C genotype with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.996 (P < 0.0001). The sensitivity (98‚Äì99%) and specificity (100%) peaked at a cutoff value of 180 ng/mL for men and 90 ng/mL for women. In a haplotype‚Äêbased analysis, SLCO1B1*5 and *15 were associated with reduced, and SLCO1B1 *1B, *14, and *35 with increased OATP1B1 function. In vitro, both GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G showed at least 6 times higher uptake by OATP1B1 than OATP1B3 or OATP2B1. These data indicate that the hepatic uptake of GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G is predominantly mediated by OATP1B1. GCDCA‚Äê3G, in particular, is a highly sensitive and specific OATP1B1 biomarker in humans.
    16‚Üí
    17‚Üí‚òë Glycochenodeoxycholate 3‚ÄêO‚Äêglucuronide (GCDCA‚Äê3G) and glycodeoxycholate 3‚ÄêO‚Äêglucuronide (GDCA‚Äê3G) are circulating OATP substrates and promising candidates as endogenous biomarkers for organic anion transporting polypeptide (OATP)‚Äìmediated drug‚Äìdrug interaction (DDI) risk assessment. The plasma concentrations of GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G have been shown to increase in response to a nonspecific OATP inhibitor and to correlate with the exposure to OATP1B probe drugs.
    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí‚òë Which of the hepatic influx transporters are responsible for GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G uptake? Are GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G sensitive endogenous biomarkers for OATB1B1 phenotyping *in vivo* in humans?
    20‚Üí
    21‚Üí‚òë GCDCA‚Äê3G is a highly sensitive and specific endogenous biomarker for identifying impaired OATP1B1 activity. GDCA‚Äê3G is somewhat less sensitive than GCDCA‚Äê3G, but also shows relatively high specificity. In healthy volunteers, the plasma GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G levels are higher in men than in women.
    22‚Üí
    23‚Üí‚òë GCDCA‚Äê3G could be a useful biomarker for assessing OATP1B1‚Äêmediated DDI risk and evaluating the mechanisms of complex DDIs. Importantly, it presents as the most sensitive OATP1B1 phenotyping biomarker to date.
    24‚Üí
    25‚ÜíPharmacokinetic drug‚Äìdrug interactions (DDIs) mediated by drug‚Äêmetabolizing enzymes and drug transporters are an important cause of adverse effects and treatment failures.[1](#cpt2053-bib-0001) Such disadvantageous drug reactions commonly occur when a perpetrator drug inhibits the metabolism or the transport of a victim drug.[2](#cpt2053-bib-0002) Consequently, prior to human dosing, new drug candidates are routinely evaluated *in vitro* as inhibitors of various drug‚Äêmetabolizing enzymes and transporters. If the evaluated risks exceed regulatory guidance thresholds, it is recommended to carry out a thorough assessment of the propensity of an investigational drug to cause DDIs *in vivo* in humans.[3](#cpt2053-bib-0003), [4](#cpt2053-bib-0004)
    26‚Üí
    27‚ÜíLimitations in preclinical transporter assays and *in vitro*‚Äìto‚Äì*in vivo* extrapolations may lead to false positive predictions and potentially unnecessary clinical DDI studies.[5](#cpt2053-bib-0005) In this regard, endogenous transporter substrates have the potential to aid in assessing the risk of transporter‚Äêmediated DDIs during early clinical drug development. Hence, there has been a growing interest in validating endogenous biomarkers for specific drug transporters, in order to substitute or supplement formal clinical DDI trials.[6](#cpt2053-bib-0006) Recently, significant progress has been made, in particular, in the assessment of endogenous biomarkers for the hepatic organic anion transporting polypeptide 1B (OATP1B) transporters.[7](#cpt2053-bib-0007), [8](#cpt2053-bib-0008), [9](#cpt2053-bib-0009), [10](#cpt2053-bib-0010), [11](#cpt2053-bib-0011), [12](#cpt2053-bib-0012) For example, biomarkers such as coproporphyrin I, hexadecanedioate, tetradecanedioate, glycochenodeoxycholate 3‚Äêsulfate (GCDCA‚ÄêS), glycodeoxycholate 3‚Äêsulfate (GDCA‚ÄêS), glycodeoxycholate 3‚ÄêO‚Äêglucuronide (GDCA‚Äê3G), glycochenodeoxycholate 3‚ÄêO‚Äêglucuronide (GCDCA‚Äê3G), and direct bilirubin have shown increased exposures after administration of the nonselective OATP1B inhibitor rifampin (international nonproprietary name, rifampicin).[7](#cpt2053-bib-0007), [8](#cpt2053-bib-0008), [9](#cpt2053-bib-0009), [10](#cpt2053-bib-0010)
    28‚Üí
    29‚ÜíIn humans, the OATP1B subfamily consists of two closely related transporters, OATP1B1 and OATP1B3. They are both exclusively expressed on the sinusoidal membrane of hepatocytes and play a crucial role in the hepatic uptake of a variety of drugs.[13](#cpt2053-bib-0013), [14](#cpt2053-bib-0014), [15](#cpt2053-bib-0015), [16](#cpt2053-bib-0016), [17](#cpt2053-bib-0017), [18](#cpt2053-bib-0018) OATP1B1 and OATP1B3 are known to mediate clinically significant DDIs. In addition, a common single‚Äênucleotide variation (SNV), c.521T>C (rs4149056, p.Val174Ala), in the *SLCO1B1* gene impairs OATP1B1 function, resulting in markedly elevated plasma levels of many drugs, such as statins.[18](#cpt2053-bib-0018), [19](#cpt2053-bib-0019), [20](#cpt2053-bib-0020), [21](#cpt2053-bib-0021), [22](#cpt2053-bib-0022), [23](#cpt2053-bib-0023), [24](#cpt2053-bib-0024), [25](#cpt2053-bib-0025), [26](#cpt2053-bib-0026), [27](#cpt2053-bib-0027)
    30‚Üí
    31‚ÜíBesides plasma drug concentrations, the reduced function *SLCO1B1* c.521T>C SNV may also raise the levels of circulating endogenous substrates, offering an additional tool in biomarker research. This was successfully demonstrated in a metabolomics and genome‚Äêwide association study, which identified potential OATP1B1 biomarkers, including hexadecanedioate and tetradecanedioate and many partly unidentified biomarker candidates.[28](#cpt2053-bib-0028) Thereafter, a few studies have investigated the effects of *SLCO1B1* genotype on endogenous biomarkers in healthy volunteers, but with limited statistical power due to small sample sizes.[29](#cpt2053-bib-0029), [30](#cpt2053-bib-0030)
    32‚Üí
    33‚ÜíUp until recently, the only bile acid glucuronide known to present as an OATP substrate was the 24‚ÄêO‚Äêglucuronide of chenodeoxycholic acid.[8](#cpt2053-bib-0008) However, GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G have also been identified as candidate biomarkers for OATP‚Äêmediated DDIs.[10](#cpt2053-bib-0010) Compared with nonglucuronidated amidated (glycine or taurine conjugated) bile acids, and even chenodeoxycholic acid 24‚ÄêO‚Äêglucuronide, both GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G exhibit robust dose‚Äêdependent responses to the OATP1B inhibitor rifampin, and their plasma levels correlate with the pharmacokinetics of OATP1B substrate drugs.[10](#cpt2053-bib-0010) These data suggest that both glucuronides could serve as endogenous biomarkers for OATP1B transporters, but further assessments are warranted to elucidate their specificity and sensitivity regarding OATP1B1 and OATP1B3. Therefore, the aim of this study was to investigate the specificities and sensitivities of GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G as OATP1B1 substrates in humans using a comprehensive genomic analysis in healthy volunteers. In addition, both glucuronides were assessed as OATP substrates *in vitro*.
    34‚Üí
    35‚Üí## Methods
    36‚Üí
    37‚Üí### Study participants and samples
    38‚Üí
    39‚ÜíA total of 356 healthy unrelated Finnish White subjects (183 women, 173 men) participated in the study after giving a written informed consent. The mean ¬± standard deviation age of the participants was 24.1 ¬± 4.1 years, weight 69.7 ¬± 12.1 kg, and body mass index 22.9 ¬± 2.7. Blood samples for measuring endogenous compounds were collected as part of a previously published[25](#cpt2053-bib-0025) and a yet unpublished single dose pharmacokinetic study (EudraCT numbers 2011‚Äê004645‚Äê40 and 2015‚Äê000540‚Äê41). Following an overnight fast at 7‚Äì8 am and before the study drug administration, a 10 mL blood sample was collected from each participant into a light‚Äêprotected ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid‚Äêcontaining tube. Because the samples were collected before the study drug administration, it is not possible that the administered drugs could have affected the concentrations of GCDCA‚Äê3G or GDCA‚Äê3G. The tube was placed on ice immediately after sampling, and plasma was separated within 30 minutes. The plasma samples were stored at ‚àí80¬∞C until analysis. The study protocols were approved by the coordinating ethics committee of the Hospital District of Helsinki and Uusimaa (Helsinki, Finland).
    40‚Üí
    41‚Üí### Chemicals
    42‚Üí
    43‚ÜíGlycochenodeoxycholate (GCDCA), glycodeoxycholate (GDCA), stable labeled GCDCA D7, GDCA D5, GCDCA‚Äê3G D4, and nonlabeled GCDCA‚Äê3G and GCDCA‚Äê3G were purchased from Toronto Research Chemicals (Toronto, Canada). Stable labeled GDCA‚Äê3G C13N15 was prepared at Pfizer (Supplemental Methods). Other chemicals and organic solvents were of commercially available analytical grade.
    44‚Üí
    45‚Üí### Quantification of plasma GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G
    46‚Üí
    47‚ÜíPlasma samples were thawed at room temperature and prepared by protein precipitation on a 96‚Äêwell Impact filter plate (Phenomenex, Torrance, CA) using a Freedom EVO automated liquid handling system (Tecan Group, M√§nnedorf, Swizerland). In short, 100 ¬µL aliquot of plasma and 400 ¬µL of acetonitrile, which contained stable isotope‚Äêlabeled internal standards (25 ng/mL each), were mixed on the filter plate. The sample mixture was allowed to incubate for 15 minutes before the supernatant was separated. The sample extract was dried using a GeneVac centrifugal evaporator (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA), followed by reconstitution in 50 ¬µL of 50% methanol. An aliquot of 1‚Äì2 ¬µL was delivered into a Sciex 6500 QTRAP + liquid chromatography‚Äêtandem mass spectrometry system (Sciex, Toronto, Canada). The simultaneous quantification of plasma GCDCA‚Äê3G, GDCA‚Äê3G, GCDCA, and GDCA were performed as previously described.[31](#cpt2053-bib-0031) The lower limits of quantification were 0.25 ng/mL, and the interday coefficient of variations were below 10% at relevant concentrations for all analytes.
    48‚Üí
    49‚Üí### Genotyping
    50‚Üí
    51‚ÜíGenomic DNA was extracted from ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid anticoagulated blood samples using the Maxwell 16 LEV Blood DNA Kit on a Maxwell 16 Research automated nucleic acid extraction system (Promega, Madison, WI). The participants were genotyped using either the Illumina Infinium Core Exome (*n* = 183) or Global Screening Array (*n* = 173) microchips at the Technology Center at the Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland (Helsinki, Finland). Only the SNVs, which were available on both chips and had minor allele frequencies over 0.01, were included in the analyses. Success rate of 97% and Hardy‚ÄêWeinberg equilibrium *P* < 10^‚àí5^ were employed as quality thresholds for including genotype data in statistical analysis. In addition, the participants were genotyped for the *SLCO1B1* c.388A>G (rs2306283, p.N130D), c.463C>A (rs11045819, p.P155T), c.521T>C, and c.1929A>C (rs34671512, p.L643F) SNVs with TaqMan genotyping assays on a QuantStudio 12K Flex Real‚ÄêTime PCR System (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The *SLCO1B1* haplotypes **1A* (c.388A‚Äêc.463C‚Äêc.521T‚Äêc.1929A), **1B* (c.388G‚Äêc.463C‚Äêc.521T‚Äêc.1929A), **5* (c.388A‚Äêc.463C‚Äêc.521C‚Äêc.1929A), **14* (c.388G‚Äêc.463A‚Äêc.521T‚Äêc.1929A), **15* (c.388G‚Äêc.463C‚Äêc.521C‚Äêc.1929A), and **35* (c.388G‚Äêc.463C‚Äêc.521T‚Äêc.1929C) were computed with PHASE v2.1.1.[32](#cpt2053-bib-0032), [33](#cpt2053-bib-0033), [34](#cpt2053-bib-0034), [35](#cpt2053-bib-0035), [36](#cpt2053-bib-0036)
    52‚Üí
    53‚Üí### Statistical analysis
    54‚Üí
    55‚ÜíThe data were analyzed with the statistical programs JMP Genomics 8.0 (SAS Institute, Inc., Cary, NC) and IBM SPSS 25 for Windows (Armonk, NY). The concentrations of GCDCA‚Äê3G, GDCA‚Äê3G, GCDCA, and GDCA as well as the glucuronide/parent compound ratios were logarithmically transformed before analysis. Possible effects of demographic covariates (sex and logarithmically transformed bodyweight and body mass index) were investigated using a forward stepwise linear regression analysis with *P* value thresholds of 0.05 for entry and 0.10 for removal as the stepping method criteria. Genome‚Äêwide association analyses were carried out using linear regression analysis with additive coding and the significant demographic covariates set as fixed factors. A *P* value of below 5 √ó 10^‚àí8^ was considered genome‚Äêwide significant. For genome‚Äêwide significant SNVs and for haplotypes of the genome‚Äêwide significant genes, analysis of variance adjusting for significant demographic covariates was carried out with pairwise comparisons with the Fisher's Least Significant Difference method. A *P* value of below 0.05 was considered statistically significant. Possible correlations of fasting plasma GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G concentrations with 3R,5S‚Äêfluvastatin area under the concentration‚Äêtime curve (AUC) were investigated using partial correlation analysis controlling for body surface area and cytochrome P450 2C9 (CYP2C9) genotype in 183 of the participants with previously published pharmacokinetic data on fluvastatin.[25](#cpt2053-bib-0025) Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis was carried out with GraphPad Prism version 8.2.1 for Windows (GraphPad Software, San Diego, CA).
    56‚Üí
    57‚Üí### In vitro studies
    58‚Üí
    59‚ÜíBoth GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G were screened *in vitro* as substrates of various human liver solute carriers individually transfected into Human Embryonic Kidney (HEK) 293 cells as previously described.[37](#cpt2053-bib-0037) In this instance, however, additional uptake studies were conducted with OATP1B1*5 and OATP1B1*15 similarly transfected into HEK293 cells (provided by Yuichi Sugiyama, Tokyo University, Japan), and OATP1B1*14 transfected into HEK293 cells (acquired from Absorption Systems, Exton, PA). OATP1B1*1A expressed in HEK293 cells was prepared in‚Äêhouse as described in Supplemental Methods. Rosuvastatin (1 ¬µM) served as a positive control probe for OATP1B1*1A, OATP1B1*14, OATP1B1*5, OATP1B1*15, OATP1B3, and OATP2B1, fluvastatin (1 ¬µM) for OATP1B1*1A, OATP1B1*14, OATP1B1*5, OATP1B1*15, taurocholate (0.2 ¬µM) for sodium‚Äêtaurocholate co‚Äêtransporting polypeptide (NTCP), cyclic guanosine monophosphate (0.5 ¬µM) for organic anion transporter 2 (OAT2), and metformin (20 ¬µM) for organic cation transporter 1 (OCT1). Briefly, HEK293 cells (wild type and stably transfected with NTCP, OATP1B1 forms, OATP1B3, OATP2B1, OAT2, and OCT1) were seeded at a density of 0.5‚Äì1.2 √ó 10^5^ cells/well on BioCoat 48 or 96‚Äêwell poly‚ÄêD‚Äêlysine coated plates (Corning Inc., Corning NY), grown in Dulbecco‚Äôs modified Eagle's medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% sodium pyruvate for 48 hours at 37¬∞C, 90% relative humidity, and 5% carbon dioxide. OATP1B1 forms, OATP1B3, OATP2B1, and NTCP‚ÄêHEK293 cells were supplemented with nonessential amino acids and GlutaMAX (Thermo Fisher Scientific). OCT1‚ÄêHEK293 and OAT2‚ÄêHEK293 cells were supplemented with 1% gentamycin, 1% sodium pyruvate, and 50 ¬µg/mL hygromycin B. To obtain an uptake ratio, both bile acid glucuronides (1 ¬µM) were incubated for 2 minutes with wild‚Äêtype and transporter‚Äêtransfected HEK293 cells. For the OATP forms, uptake ratios were determined up to 5 minutes of incubation and at three different concentrations of glucuronide substrate (1, 5, and 10 ¬µM), but only 2‚Äêminute data at 1 ¬µM are reported herein. The performance of the different transporter‚Äêtransfected cells was validated using the probe substrates (incubated over 2 to 4 minutes) described above.[37](#cpt2053-bib-0037) At the end of the incubation, cellular uptake was determined by washing the cells four times with ice‚Äêcold transport buffer followed by lysis with 0.2 mL of methanol containing internal standard. Quantification of GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G in cells was as described in Supplemental Methods. Sample processing and bioanalysis of rosuvastatin, taurocholic acid, metformin, fluvastatin, and cyclic guanosine monophosphate has already been described.[37](#cpt2053-bib-0037)
    60‚Üí
    61‚ÜíBoth GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G (~ 0.01 to 100 ¬µM) were also studied as *in vitro* inhibitors of OATP1B1*1A, OATP1B3, and OATP2B1 (expressed in HEK293 cells) using rosuvastatin (0.5 ¬µM) as substrate. Where possible, the concentration of glucuronide rendering 50% inhibition of rosuvastatin uptake (IC_50_) was determined. In addition, GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G were evaluated as substrates of adenosine triphosphate (ATP)‚Äêbinding cassette (ABC) efflux transporters expressed in vesicles; human bile salt export pump (BSEP), and multidrug resistance‚Äêassociated proteins 2, 3, and 4 (MRP2, MRP3, MRP4) as described in Supplemental Methods.
    62‚Üí
    63‚Üí## Results
    64‚Üí
    65‚Üí### Genome‚Äêwide association study for plasma GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G
    66‚Üí
    67‚ÜíThe mean fasting plasma concentrations of GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G were ~ 50% lower (*P* = 2.25 √ó 10^‚àí18^ and *P* = 4.73 √ó 10^‚àí9^) and those of GCDCA and GDCA were ~ 20% lower (*P* = 0.0165 and *P* = 0.0328) in women than in men. Accordingly, the GCDCA‚Äê3G/GCDCA and GDCA‚Äê3G/GDCA concentration ratios were 42% and 32% lower in women than in men (*P* = 7.27 √ó 10^‚àí10^ and *P* = 1.50 √ó 10^‚àí6^). In a genome‚Äêwide association analysis adjusted for sex, the *SLCO1B1* rs4149056 (c.521T>C, p.Val174Ala) SNV showed the strongest associations with the plasma concentrations of both GCDCA‚Äê3G (*P* = 3.09 √ó 10^‚àí30^) and GDCA‚Äê3G (*P* = 1.60 √ó 10^‚àí17^) (**Figure ** [1](#cpt2053-fig-0001)). After adjusting for the c.521T> C SNV, no other SNVs remained associated with GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G concentrations at the genome‚Äêwide significance level. In subjects homozygous for the *SLCO1B1* c.521T>C SNV, the mean plasma concentrations of GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G were 9.2‚Äêfold and 6.4‚Äêfold of those in individuals with the T/T genotype (*P* = 8.87 √ó 10^‚àí31^ and *P* = 2.45 √ó 10^‚àí13^) (**Table ** [1](#cpt2053-tbl-0001), **Table ** [S1](#cpt2053-sup-0003), **Figure ** [2](#cpt2053-fig-0002)). In T/C heterozygotes, the mean plasma concentrations of GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G were 1.7‚Äêfold and 1.9‚Äêfold of those in individuals with the T/T genotype (*P* = 1.21 √ó 10^‚àí13^ and *P* = 1.35 √ó 10^‚àí9^). The *SLCO1B1* c.521T>C SNV was not significantly associated with the plasma concentrations of parent GCDCA or GDCA, but their concentrations tended to be slightly increased in C/C homozygotes. In line with these results, the plasma GCDCA‚Äê3G to GCDCA and GDCA‚Äê3G to GDCA concentration ratios were markedly increased in C/C homozygotes compared with T/T homozygotes, but the increases were slightly smaller than those of the respective glucuronide concentrations (**Table ** [1](#cpt2053-tbl-0001), **Table ** [S1](#cpt2053-sup-0003)). Moreover, GCDCA‚Äê3G (*r* = 0.218, *P* = 0.003), but not GDCA‚Äê3G (*r* = 0.108, *P* = 0.149) concentration showed a positive correlation with 3R,5S‚Äêfluvastatin AUC.
    68‚Üí
    69‚Üí### Figure 1.
    70‚Üí
    71‚Üí![Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/045a/7983942/89a00517ce97/CPT-109-646-g002.jpg)
    72‚Üí
    73‚ÜíManhattan plots of the fasting plasma concentrations of (a) GCDCA‚Äê3G and (b) GDCA‚Äê3G. Horizontal lines indicate the genome‚Äêwide significance level of 5 √ó 10‚àí8.
    74‚Üí
    75‚Üí### Table 1.
    76‚Üí
    77‚Üí| Variable | T/T genotype (control; n = 230) | T/C genotype (n = 113) |  |  | C/C genotype (n = 13) |  |  |
    78‚Üí| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
    79‚Üí| Geometric mean (95% CI) | Geometric mean (95% CI) | Ratio to control (95% CI) | P | Geometric mean (95% CI) | Ratio to control (95% CI) | P |  |
    80‚Üí| GCDCA‚Äê3G (ng/mL) | 25.8 (23.8, 28.0) | 44.6 (39.8, 50.0) | 1.73 (1.50, 1.99) | 1.21 √ó 10‚àí13 | 238 (171, 333) | 9.23 (6.55, 13.0) | 8.77 √ó 10‚àí31 |
    81‚Üí| GDCA‚Äê3G (ng/mL) | 56.9 (51.0, 63.7) | 106 (90.0, 124) | 1.85 (1.53, 2.25) | 1.35 √ó 10‚àí9 | 367 (229, 585) | 6.43 (3.98, 10.4) | 2.45 √ó 10‚àí13 |
    82‚Üí| GCDCA (ng/mL) | 419 (378, 465) | 378 (326, 438) | 0.900 (0.751, 1.08) | 0.252 | 567 (367, 876) | 1.35 (0.865, 2.11) | 0.185 |
    83‚Üí| GDCA (ng/mL) | 122 (107, 138) | 124 (104, 149) | 1.02 (0.822, 1.27) | 0.839 | 141 (83.3, 237) | 1.16 (0.675, 1.98) | 0.598 |
    84‚Üí| GCDCA‚Äê3G/GCDCA ratio | 0.0615 (0.0563, 0.0673) | 0.118 (0.104, 0.134) | 1.92 (1.64, 2.24) | 3.55 √ó 10‚àí15 | 0.420 (0.289, 0.611) | 6.83 (4.65, 10.0) | 2.51 √ó 10‚àí20 |
    85‚Üí| GDCA‚Äê3G/GDCA ratio | 0.468 (0.431, 0.509) | 0.848 (0.753, 0.955) | 1.81 (1.57, 2.10) | 1.49 √ó 10‚àí14 | 2.61 (1.84, 3.70) | 5.57 (3.89, 7.98) | 7.00 √ó 10‚àí19 |
    86‚Üí
    87‚ÜíTable 1 Caption: Effects of the SLCO1B1 c.521T>C single nucleotide variation on the plasma concentrations of GCDCA‚Äê3G, GDCA‚Äê3G, GCDCA, and GDCA
    88‚Üí
    89‚Üí### Figure 2.
    90‚Üí
    91‚Üí![Figure 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/045a/7983942/4ac8220fb97f/CPT-109-646-g003.jpg)
    92‚Üí
    93‚ÜíEffects of SLCO1B1 c.521T>C single nucleotide variation on the plasma concentrations of (a) GCDCA‚Äê3G and (b) GDCA‚Äê3G, (c) the ratio of GCDCA‚Äê3G to GCDCA, and (d) the ratio of GDCA‚Äê3G to GDCA. Horizontal lines indicate geometric estimated marginal mean values and whiskers indicate 95% confidence intervals. Triangles, women; circles, men.
    94‚Üí
    95‚Üí### Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis
    96‚Üí
    97‚ÜíTo quantify the sensitivity and specificity of GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G to detect reduced OATP1B1 activity, we calculated the areas under the ROC curves (AUROCs) and determined the cutoff values for the optimal discrimination of the C/C genotype from the T/C and T/T genotypes (**Figure** [S1](#cpt2053-sup-0001)). The AUROC of GCDCA‚Äê3G was larger than 0.99 for both men and women (*P* ‚â§ 0.0002), and the cutoff values with the highest true positive rate together with the lowest false positive rate were 180 and 90 ng/mL, respectively. With these cutoff values, the sensitivity was 100% and specificity higher than 98%. For GDCA‚Äê3G, the AUROCs for both sexes were larger than 0.85 (*P* ‚â§ 0.0071) and the optimal cutoff values (430 and 280 ng/mL) resulted in 75‚Äì80% sensitivity and 97% specificity. GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G separated C/C homozygotes from the T/C heterozygotes nearly as well as from the T/C and T/T genotypes, but the separation between T/C heterozygotes and T/T homozygotes was not as clear (**Figure** [S1](#cpt2053-sup-0001)).
    98‚Üí
    99‚Üí### Haplotype analysis
   100‚Üí

# Identification of Glycochenodeoxycholate 3‚ÄêO‚ÄêGlucuronide and Glycodeoxycholate 3‚ÄêO‚ÄêGlucuronide as Highly Sensitive and Specific OATP1B1 Biomarkers

## Metadata
**Authors:** Mikko Neuvonen, P√§ivi Hirvensalo, Aleksi Tornio, Brian Rago, Mark West, Sarah Lazzaro, Sumathy Mathialagan, Manthena Varma, Matthew A Cerny, Chester Costales, Ragu Ramanathan, A David Rodrigues, Mikko Niemi
**Journal:** Clinical Pharmacology and Therapeutics
**Date:** 2020 Oct 18
**DOI:** [10.1002/cpt.2053](https://doi.org/10.1002/cpt.2053)
**PMID:** 32961594
**PMCID:** PMC7983942
**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7983942/
**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7983942/pdf/CPT-109-646.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7983942/pdf/CPT-109-646.pdf)

## Abstract

The aim of this study was to investigate the sensitivity and specificity of endogenous glycochenodeoxycholate and glycodeoxycholate 3‚ÄêO‚Äêglucuronides (GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G) as substrates for organic anion transporting polypeptide 1B1 (OATP1B1) in humans. We measured fasting levels of plasma GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G using liquid chromatography‚Äêtandem mass spectrometry in 356 healthy volunteers. The mean plasma levels of both compounds were ~ 50% lower in women than in men (P = 2.25 √ó 10‚àí18 and P = 4.73 √ó 10‚àí9). In a microarray‚Äêbased genome‚Äêwide association study, the SLCO1B1 rs4149056 (c.521T>C, p.Val174Ala) variation showed the strongest association with the plasma GCDCA‚Äê3G (P = 3.09 √ó 10‚àí30) and GDCA‚Äê3G (P = 1.60 √ó 10‚àí17) concentrations. The mean plasma concentration of GCDCA‚Äê3G was 9.2‚Äêfold (P = 8.77 √ó 10‚àí31) and that of GDCA‚Äê3G was 6.4‚Äêfold (P = 2.45x10‚àí13) higher in individuals with the SLCO1B1 c.521C/C genotype than in those with the c.521T/T genotype. No other variants showed independent genome‚Äêwide significant associations with GCDCA‚Äê3G or GDCA‚Äê3G. GCDCA‚Äê3G was highly efficacious in detecting the SLCO1B1 c.521C/C genotype with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.996 (P < 0.0001). The sensitivity (98‚Äì99%) and specificity (100%) peaked at a cutoff value of 180 ng/mL for men and 90 ng/mL for women. In a haplotype‚Äêbased analysis, SLCO1B1*5 and *15 were associated with reduced, and SLCO1B1 *1B, *14, and *35 with increased OATP1B1 function. In vitro, both GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G showed at least 6 times higher uptake by OATP1B1 than OATP1B3 or OATP2B1. These data indicate that the hepatic uptake of GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G is predominantly mediated by OATP1B1. GCDCA‚Äê3G, in particular, is a highly sensitive and specific OATP1B1 biomarker in humans.

‚òë Glycochenodeoxycholate 3‚ÄêO‚Äêglucuronide (GCDCA‚Äê3G) and glycodeoxycholate 3‚ÄêO‚Äêglucuronide (GDCA‚Äê3G) are circulating OATP substrates and promising candidates as endogenous biomarkers for organic anion transporting polypeptide (OATP)‚Äìmediated drug‚Äìdrug interaction (DDI) risk assessment. The plasma concentrations of GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G have been shown to increase in response to a nonspecific OATP inhibitor and to correlate with the exposure to OATP1B probe drugs.

‚òë Which of the hepatic influx transporters are responsible for GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G uptake? Are GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G sensitive endogenous biomarkers for OATB1B1 phenotyping *in vivo* in humans?

‚òë GCDCA‚Äê3G is a highly sensitive and specific endogenous biomarker for identifying impaired OATP1B1 activity. GDCA‚Äê3G is somewhat less sensitive than GCDCA‚Äê3G, but also shows relatively high specificity. In healthy volunteers, the plasma GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G levels are higher in men than in women.

‚òë GCDCA‚Äê3G could be a useful biomarker for assessing OATP1B1‚Äêmediated DDI risk and evaluating the mechanisms of complex DDIs. Importantly, it presents as the most sensitive OATP1B1 phenotyping biomarker to date.

Pharmacokinetic drug‚Äìdrug interactions (DDIs) mediated by drug‚Äêmetabolizing enzymes and drug transporters are an important cause of adverse effects and treatment failures.[1](#cpt2053-bib-0001) Such disadvantageous drug reactions commonly occur when a perpetrator drug inhibits the metabolism or the transport of a victim drug.[2](#cpt2053-bib-0002) Consequently, prior to human dosing, new drug candidates are routinely evaluated *in vitro* as inhibitors of various drug‚Äêmetabolizing enzymes and transporters. If the evaluated risks exceed regulatory guidance thresholds, it is recommended to carry out a thorough assessment of the propensity of an investigational drug to cause DDIs *in vivo* in humans.[3](#cpt2053-bib-0003), [4](#cpt2053-bib-0004)

Limitations in preclinical transporter assays and *in vitro*‚Äìto‚Äì*in vivo* extrapolations may lead to false positive predictions and potentially unnecessary clinical DDI studies.[5](#cpt2053-bib-0005) In this regard, endogenous transporter substrates have the potential to aid in assessing the risk of transporter‚Äêmediated DDIs during early clinical drug development. Hence, there has been a growing interest in validating endogenous biomarkers for specific drug transporters, in order to substitute or supplement formal clinical DDI trials.[6](#cpt2053-bib-0006) Recently, significant progress has been made, in particular, in the assessment of endogenous biomarkers for the hepatic organic anion transporting polypeptide 1B (OATP1B) transporters.[7](#cpt2053-bib-0007), [8](#cpt2053-bib-0008), [9](#cpt2053-bib-0009), [10](#cpt2053-bib-0010), [11](#cpt2053-bib-0011), [12](#cpt2053-bib-0012) For example, biomarkers such as coproporphyrin I, hexadecanedioate, tetradecanedioate, glycochenodeoxycholate 3‚Äêsulfate (GCDCA‚ÄêS), glycodeoxycholate 3‚Äêsulfate (GDCA‚ÄêS), glycodeoxycholate 3‚ÄêO‚Äêglucuronide (GDCA‚Äê3G), glycochenodeoxycholate 3‚ÄêO‚Äêglucuronide (GCDCA‚Äê3G), and direct bilirubin have shown increased exposures after administration of the nonselective OATP1B inhibitor rifampin (international nonproprietary name, rifampicin).[7](#cpt2053-bib-0007), [8](#cpt2053-bib-0008), [9](#cpt2053-bib-0009), [10](#cpt2053-bib-0010)

In humans, the OATP1B subfamily consists of two closely related transporters, OATP1B1 and OATP1B3. They are both exclusively expressed on the sinusoidal membrane of hepatocytes and play a crucial role in the hepatic uptake of a variety of drugs.[13](#cpt2053-bib-0013), [14](#cpt2053-bib-0014), [15](#cpt2053-bib-0015), [16](#cpt2053-bib-0016), [17](#cpt2053-bib-0017), [18](#cpt2053-bib-0018) OATP1B1 and OATP1B3 are known to mediate clinically significant DDIs. In addition, a common single‚Äênucleotide variation (SNV), c.521T>C (rs4149056, p.Val174Ala), in the *SLCO1B1* gene impairs OATP1B1 function, resulting in markedly elevated plasma levels of many drugs, such as statins.[18](#cpt2053-bib-0018), [19](#cpt2053-bib-0019), [20](#cpt2053-bib-0020), [21](#cpt2053-bib-0021), [22](#cpt2053-bib-0022), [23](#cpt2053-bib-0023), [24](#cpt2053-bib-0024), [25](#cpt2053-bib-0025), [26](#cpt2053-bib-0026), [27](#cpt2053-bib-0027)

Besides plasma drug concentrations, the reduced function *SLCO1B1* c.521T>C SNV may also raise the levels of circulating endogenous substrates, offering an additional tool in biomarker research. This was successfully demonstrated in a metabolomics and genome‚Äêwide association study, which identified potential OATP1B1 biomarkers, including hexadecanedioate and tetradecanedioate and many partly unidentified biomarker candidates.[28](#cpt2053-bib-0028) Thereafter, a few studies have investigated the effects of *SLCO1B1* genotype on endogenous biomarkers in healthy volunteers, but with limited statistical power due to small sample sizes.[29](#cpt2053-bib-0029), [30](#cpt2053-bib-0030)

Up until recently, the only bile acid glucuronide known to present as an OATP substrate was the 24‚ÄêO‚Äêglucuronide of chenodeoxycholic acid.[8](#cpt2053-bib-0008) However, GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G have also been identified as candidate biomarkers for OATP‚Äêmediated DDIs.[10](#cpt2053-bib-0010) Compared with nonglucuronidated amidated (glycine or taurine conjugated) bile acids, and even chenodeoxycholic acid 24‚ÄêO‚Äêglucuronide, both GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G exhibit robust dose‚Äêdependent responses to the OATP1B inhibitor rifampin, and their plasma levels correlate with the pharmacokinetics of OATP1B substrate drugs.[10](#cpt2053-bib-0010) These data suggest that both glucuronides could serve as endogenous biomarkers for OATP1B transporters, but further assessments are warranted to elucidate their specificity and sensitivity regarding OATP1B1 and OATP1B3. Therefore, the aim of this study was to investigate the specificities and sensitivities of GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G as OATP1B1 substrates in humans using a comprehensive genomic analysis in healthy volunteers. In addition, both glucuronides were assessed as OATP substrates *in vitro*.

## Methods

### Study participants and samples

A total of 356 healthy unrelated Finnish White subjects (183 women, 173 men) participated in the study after giving a written informed consent. The mean ¬± standard deviation age of the participants was 24.1 ¬± 4.1 years, weight 69.7 ¬± 12.1 kg, and body mass index 22.9 ¬± 2.7. Blood samples for measuring endogenous compounds were collected as part of a previously published[25](#cpt2053-bib-0025) and a yet unpublished single dose pharmacokinetic study (EudraCT numbers 2011‚Äê004645‚Äê40 and 2015‚Äê000540‚Äê41). Following an overnight fast at 7‚Äì8 am and before the study drug administration, a 10 mL blood sample was collected from each participant into a light‚Äêprotected ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid‚Äêcontaining tube. Because the samples were collected before the study drug administration, it is not possible that the administered drugs could have affected the concentrations of GCDCA‚Äê3G or GDCA‚Äê3G. The tube was placed on ice immediately after sampling, and plasma was separated within 30 minutes. The plasma samples were stored at ‚àí80¬∞C until analysis. The study protocols were approved by the coordinating ethics committee of the Hospital District of Helsinki and Uusimaa (Helsinki, Finland).

### Chemicals

Glycochenodeoxycholate (GCDCA), glycodeoxycholate (GDCA), stable labeled GCDCA D7, GDCA D5, GCDCA‚Äê3G D4, and nonlabeled GCDCA‚Äê3G and GCDCA‚Äê3G were purchased from Toronto Research Chemicals (Toronto, Canada). Stable labeled GDCA‚Äê3G C13N15 was prepared at Pfizer (Supplemental Methods). Other chemicals and organic solvents were of commercially available analytical grade.

### Quantification of plasma GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G

Plasma samples were thawed at room temperature and prepared by protein precipitation on a 96‚Äêwell Impact filter plate (Phenomenex, Torrance, CA) using a Freedom EVO automated liquid handling system (Tecan Group, M√§nnedorf, Swizerland). In short, 100 ¬µL aliquot of plasma and 400 ¬µL of acetonitrile, which contained stable isotope‚Äêlabeled internal standards (25 ng/mL each), were mixed on the filter plate. The sample mixture was allowed to incubate for 15 minutes before the supernatant was separated. The sample extract was dried using a GeneVac centrifugal evaporator (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA), followed by reconstitution in 50 ¬µL of 50% methanol. An aliquot of 1‚Äì2 ¬µL was delivered into a Sciex 6500 QTRAP + liquid chromatography‚Äêtandem mass spectrometry system (Sciex, Toronto, Canada). The simultaneous quantification of plasma GCDCA‚Äê3G, GDCA‚Äê3G, GCDCA, and GDCA were performed as previously described.[31](#cpt2053-bib-0031) The lower limits of quantification were 0.25 ng/mL, and the interday coefficient of variations were below 10% at relevant concentrations for all analytes.

### Genotyping

Genomic DNA was extracted from ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid anticoagulated blood samples using the Maxwell 16 LEV Blood DNA Kit on a Maxwell 16 Research automated nucleic acid extraction system (Promega, Madison, WI). The participants were genotyped using either the Illumina Infinium Core Exome (*n* = 183) or Global Screening Array (*n* = 173) microchips at the Technology Center at the Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland (Helsinki, Finland). Only the SNVs, which were available on both chips and had minor allele frequencies over 0.01, were included in the analyses. Success rate of 97% and Hardy‚ÄêWeinberg equilibrium *P* < 10^‚àí5^ were employed as quality thresholds for including genotype data in statistical analysis. In addition, the participants were genotyped for the *SLCO1B1* c.388A>G (rs2306283, p.N130D), c.463C>A (rs11045819, p.P155T), c.521T>C, and c.1929A>C (rs34671512, p.L643F) SNVs with TaqMan genotyping assays on a QuantStudio 12K Flex Real‚ÄêTime PCR System (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The *SLCO1B1* haplotypes **1A* (c.388A‚Äêc.463C‚Äêc.521T‚Äêc.1929A), **1B* (c.388G‚Äêc.463C‚Äêc.521T‚Äêc.1929A), **5* (c.388A‚Äêc.463C‚Äêc.521C‚Äêc.1929A), **14* (c.388G‚Äêc.463A‚Äêc.521T‚Äêc.1929A), **15* (c.388G‚Äêc.463C‚Äêc.521C‚Äêc.1929A), and **35* (c.388G‚Äêc.463C‚Äêc.521T‚Äêc.1929C) were computed with PHASE v2.1.1.[32](#cpt2053-bib-0032), [33](#cpt2053-bib-0033), [34](#cpt2053-bib-0034), [35](#cpt2053-bib-0035), [36](#cpt2053-bib-0036)

### Statistical analysis

The data were analyzed with the statistical programs JMP Genomics 8.0 (SAS Institute, Inc., Cary, NC) and IBM SPSS 25 for Windows (Armonk, NY). The concentrations of GCDCA‚Äê3G, GDCA‚Äê3G, GCDCA, and GDCA as well as the glucuronide/parent compound ratios were logarithmically transformed before analysis. Possible effects of demographic covariates (sex and logarithmically transformed bodyweight and body mass index) were investigated using a forward stepwise linear regression analysis with *P* value thresholds of 0.05 for entry and 0.10 for removal as the stepping method criteria. Genome‚Äêwide association analyses were carried out using linear regression analysis with additive coding and the significant demographic covariates set as fixed factors. A *P* value of below 5 √ó 10^‚àí8^ was considered genome‚Äêwide significant. For genome‚Äêwide significant SNVs and for haplotypes of the genome‚Äêwide significant genes, analysis of variance adjusting for significant demographic covariates was carried out with pairwise comparisons with the Fisher's Least Significant Difference method. A *P* value of below 0.05 was considered statistically significant. Possible correlations of fasting plasma GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G concentrations with 3R,5S‚Äêfluvastatin area under the concentration‚Äêtime curve (AUC) were investigated using partial correlation analysis controlling for body surface area and cytochrome P450 2C9 (CYP2C9) genotype in 183 of the participants with previously published pharmacokinetic data on fluvastatin.[25](#cpt2053-bib-0025) Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis was carried out with GraphPad Prism version 8.2.1 for Windows (GraphPad Software, San Diego, CA).

### In vitro studies

Both GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G were screened *in vitro* as substrates of various human liver solute carriers individually transfected into Human Embryonic Kidney (HEK) 293 cells as previously described.[37](#cpt2053-bib-0037) In this instance, however, additional uptake studies were conducted with OATP1B1*5 and OATP1B1*15 similarly transfected into HEK293 cells (provided by Yuichi Sugiyama, Tokyo University, Japan), and OATP1B1*14 transfected into HEK293 cells (acquired from Absorption Systems, Exton, PA). OATP1B1*1A expressed in HEK293 cells was prepared in‚Äêhouse as described in Supplemental Methods. Rosuvastatin (1 ¬µM) served as a positive control probe for OATP1B1*1A, OATP1B1*14, OATP1B1*5, OATP1B1*15, OATP1B3, and OATP2B1, fluvastatin (1 ¬µM) for OATP1B1*1A, OATP1B1*14, OATP1B1*5, OATP1B1*15, taurocholate (0.2 ¬µM) for sodium‚Äêtaurocholate co‚Äêtransporting polypeptide (NTCP), cyclic guanosine monophosphate (0.5 ¬µM) for organic anion transporter 2 (OAT2), and metformin (20 ¬µM) for organic cation transporter 1 (OCT1). Briefly, HEK293 cells (wild type and stably transfected with NTCP, OATP1B1 forms, OATP1B3, OATP2B1, OAT2, and OCT1) were seeded at a density of 0.5‚Äì1.2 √ó 10^5^ cells/well on BioCoat 48 or 96‚Äêwell poly‚ÄêD‚Äêlysine coated plates (Corning Inc., Corning NY), grown in Dulbecco‚Äôs modified Eagle's medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% sodium pyruvate for 48 hours at 37¬∞C, 90% relative humidity, and 5% carbon dioxide. OATP1B1 forms, OATP1B3, OATP2B1, and NTCP‚ÄêHEK293 cells were supplemented with nonessential amino acids and GlutaMAX (Thermo Fisher Scientific). OCT1‚ÄêHEK293 and OAT2‚ÄêHEK293 cells were supplemented with 1% gentamycin, 1% sodium pyruvate, and 50 ¬µg/mL hygromycin B. To obtain an uptake ratio, both bile acid glucuronides (1 ¬µM) were incubated for 2 minutes with wild‚Äêtype and transporter‚Äêtransfected HEK293 cells. For the OATP forms, uptake ratios were determined up to 5 minutes of incubation and at three different concentrations of glucuronide substrate (1, 5, and 10 ¬µM), but only 2‚Äêminute data at 1 ¬µM are reported herein. The performance of the different transporter‚Äêtransfected cells was validated using the probe substrates (incubated over 2 to 4 minutes) described above.[37](#cpt2053-bib-0037) At the end of the incubation, cellular uptake was determined by washing the cells four times with ice‚Äêcold transport buffer followed by lysis with 0.2 mL of methanol containing internal standard. Quantification of GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G in cells was as described in Supplemental Methods. Sample processing and bioanalysis of rosuvastatin, taurocholic acid, metformin, fluvastatin, and cyclic guanosine monophosphate has already been described.[37](#cpt2053-bib-0037)

Both GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G (~ 0.01 to 100 ¬µM) were also studied as *in vitro* inhibitors of OATP1B1*1A, OATP1B3, and OATP2B1 (expressed in HEK293 cells) using rosuvastatin (0.5 ¬µM) as substrate. Where possible, the concentration of glucuronide rendering 50% inhibition of rosuvastatin uptake (IC_50_) was determined. In addition, GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G were evaluated as substrates of adenosine triphosphate (ATP)‚Äêbinding cassette (ABC) efflux transporters expressed in vesicles; human bile salt export pump (BSEP), and multidrug resistance‚Äêassociated proteins 2, 3, and 4 (MRP2, MRP3, MRP4) as described in Supplemental Methods.

## Results

### Genome‚Äêwide association study for plasma GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G

The mean fasting plasma concentrations of GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G were ~ 50% lower (*P* = 2.25 √ó 10^‚àí18^ and *P* = 4.73 √ó 10^‚àí9^) and those of GCDCA and GDCA were ~ 20% lower (*P* = 0.0165 and *P* = 0.0328) in women than in men. Accordingly, the GCDCA‚Äê3G/GCDCA and GDCA‚Äê3G/GDCA concentration ratios were 42% and 32% lower in women than in men (*P* = 7.27 √ó 10^‚àí10^ and *P* = 1.50 √ó 10^‚àí6^). In a genome‚Äêwide association analysis adjusted for sex, the *SLCO1B1* rs4149056 (c.521T>C, p.Val174Ala) SNV showed the strongest associations with the plasma concentrations of both GCDCA‚Äê3G (*P* = 3.09 √ó 10^‚àí30^) and GDCA‚Äê3G (*P* = 1.60 √ó 10^‚àí17^) (**Figure ** [1](#cpt2053-fig-0001)). After adjusting for the c.521T> C SNV, no other SNVs remained associated with GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G concentrations at the genome‚Äêwide significance level. In subjects homozygous for the *SLCO1B1* c.521T>C SNV, the mean plasma concentrations of GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G were 9.2‚Äêfold and 6.4‚Äêfold of those in individuals with the T/T genotype (*P* = 8.87 √ó 10^‚àí31^ and *P* = 2.45 √ó 10^‚àí13^) (**Table ** [1](#cpt2053-tbl-0001), **Table ** [S1](#cpt2053-sup-0003), **Figure ** [2](#cpt2053-fig-0002)). In T/C heterozygotes, the mean plasma concentrations of GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G were 1.7‚Äêfold and 1.9‚Äêfold of those in individuals with the T/T genotype (*P* = 1.21 √ó 10^‚àí13^ and *P* = 1.35 √ó 10^‚àí9^). The *SLCO1B1* c.521T>C SNV was not significantly associated with the plasma concentrations of parent GCDCA or GDCA, but their concentrations tended to be slightly increased in C/C homozygotes. In line with these results, the plasma GCDCA‚Äê3G to GCDCA and GDCA‚Äê3G to GDCA concentration ratios were markedly increased in C/C homozygotes compared with T/T homozygotes, but the increases were slightly smaller than those of the respective glucuronide concentrations (**Table ** [1](#cpt2053-tbl-0001), **Table ** [S1](#cpt2053-sup-0003)). Moreover, GCDCA‚Äê3G (*r* = 0.218, *P* = 0.003), but not GDCA‚Äê3G (*r* = 0.108, *P* = 0.149) concentration showed a positive correlation with 3R,5S‚Äêfluvastatin AUC.

### Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis

To quantify the sensitivity and specificity of GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G to detect reduced OATP1B1 activity, we calculated the areas under the ROC curves (AUROCs) and determined the cutoff values for the optimal discrimination of the C/C genotype from the T/C and T/T genotypes (**Figure** [S1](#cpt2053-sup-0001)). The AUROC of GCDCA‚Äê3G was larger than 0.99 for both men and women (*P* ‚â§ 0.0002), and the cutoff values with the highest true positive rate together with the lowest false positive rate were 180 and 90 ng/mL, respectively. With these cutoff values, the sensitivity was 100% and specificity higher than 98%. For GDCA‚Äê3G, the AUROCs for both sexes were larger than 0.85 (*P* ‚â§ 0.0071) and the optimal cutoff values (430 and 280 ng/mL) resulted in 75‚Äì80% sensitivity and 97% specificity. GCDCA‚Äê3G and GDCA‚Äê3G separated C/C homozygotes from the T/C heterozygotes nearly as well as from the T/C and T/T genotypes, but the separation between T/C heterozygotes and T/T homozygotes was not as clear (**Figure** [S1](#cpt2053-sup-0001)).

1‚Üí# Substantially Increased Plasma Coproporphyrin‚ÄêI Concentrations Associated With OATP1B1*15 Allele in Japanese General Population
     2‚Üí
     3‚Üí## Metadata
     4‚Üí**Authors:** Yosuke Suzuki, Yuri Sasamoto, Teruhide Koyama, Chisato Yoshijima, Masahiro Nakatochi, Michiaki Kubo, Yukihide Momozawa, Ritei Uehara, Keiko Ohno
     5‚Üí**Journal:** Clinical and Translational Science
     6‚Üí**Date:** 2020 Oct 5
     7‚Üí**DOI:** [10.1111/cts.12889](https://doi.org/10.1111/cts.12889)
     8‚Üí**PMID:** 32961019
     9‚Üí**PMCID:** PMC7877856
    10‚Üí**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7877856/
    11‚Üí**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7877856/pdf/CTS-14-382.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7877856/pdf/CTS-14-382.pdf)
    12‚Üí
    13‚Üí## Abstract
    14‚Üí
    15‚ÜíCoproporphyrin‚ÄêI (CP‚ÄêI) in plasma is a sensitive and specific endogenous probe for phenotyping organic anion transporting polypeptides 1B (OATP1B, encoded by SLCO1B). A few small‚Äêscale studies suggested that plasma CP‚ÄêI concentration is affected by OATP1B1 polymorphism, but detailed studies are lacking. In this large‚Äêscale study, we measured plasma CP‚ÄêI concentrations in 391 subjects from the Japanese general population, and evaluated the relationship between plasma CP‚ÄêI concentrations and OATP1B1 polymorphisms to further assess the utility of plasma CP‚ÄêI concentrations as an endogenous OATP1B probe. Plasma CP‚ÄêI concentrations were 0.45 ¬± 0.12, 0.47 ¬± 0.16, 0.47 ¬± 0.20, 0.50 ¬± 0.15, 0.54 ¬± 0.14, and 0.74 ¬± 0.31 ng/mL in participants with OATP1B1*1b/*1b (n = 103), *1a/*1b (n = 122), *1a/*1a (n = 40), *1b/*15 (n = 74), *1a/*15 (n = 41), and *15/*15 (n = 11), respectively, showing an ascending rank order with significant difference (P < 0.0001). Post hoc analysis revealed significant increases in plasma CP‚ÄêI concentration in OATP1B1*1b/*15 (P = 0.036), *1a/*15 (P = 0.0005), and *15/*15 (P = 0.0003) groups compared with the OATP1B1*1b/*1b group. There was no significant difference among OATP1B genotypes in plasma concentration of 3‚Äêcarboxy‚Äê4‚Äêmethyl‚Äê5‚Äêpropyl‚Äê2‚Äêfuranpropanoic acid, a uremic toxin reported to decrease OATP1B activity in vivo. These findings confirm the utility of plasma CP‚ÄêI concentrations as an endogenous biomarker for phenotyping of OATP1B activity. Plasma CP‚ÄêI concentration is potentially useful for the study of drug‚Äêdrug interactions via OATP1B or individual dose adjustment of OATP1B substrates.
    16‚Üí
    17‚ÜíPhenotyping of organic anion transporting polypeptides 1B (OATP1B) seems to be a useful tool for quantitative assessment of *in vivo* OATP1B activity. Several endogenous biomarkers for OATP1B phenotyping have been reported and coproporphyrin‚ÄêI (CP‚ÄêI) is especially focused as a sensitive and specific probe.
    18‚Üí
    19‚ÜíDoes *OATP1B1*15* allele, which decreases the transporting function of OATP1B1, affect plasma CP‚ÄêI concentrations in Japanese general population?
    20‚Üí
    21‚ÜíPlasma CP‚ÄêI concentrations showed an ascending rank order in subjects with *OATP1B1*1b/*1b*, **1a/*1b*, **1a/*1a*, **1b/*15*, **1a/*15*, and **15/*15*, with significant difference. *Post hoc* analysis revealed significant increases in plasma CP‚ÄêI concentrations in *OATP1B1*1b/*15*, **1a/*15*, and **15/*15* groups compared with the *OATP1B1*1b/*1b* group. Especially, substantial increase was observed in *OATP1B1*15/*15*.
    22‚Üí
    23‚ÜíThe results further substantiated the utility of plasma CP‚ÄêI concentrations as an endogenous biomarker for phenotyping OATP1B activity. Plasma CP‚ÄêI concentrations can be utilized in studies of drug‚Äêdrug interactions via OATP1B and individual dose adjustment of OATP1B substrates.
    24‚Üí
    25‚ÜíDrug transporters are localized at cell membranes and involved in drug transport in various tissues such as the liver, kidneys, gastrointestinal tract, and brain endothelium. Organic anion transporting polypeptides 1B (OATP1B, encoded by *SLCO1B*) is a hepatic uptake transporter that substantially affect pharmacokinetics of some drugs, such as hydroxymethylglutaryl (HMG)‚ÄêCoA reductase inhibitors and some anti‚Äêhepatitis C virus drugs.[1](#cts12889-bib-0001), [2](#cts12889-bib-0002), [3](#cts12889-bib-0003), [4](#cts12889-bib-0004), [5](#cts12889-bib-0005), [6](#cts12889-bib-0006) *In vivo* OATP1B activity has large interindividual variability and is affected by environmental, physiologic, and genetic factors. For example, OATP inhibitors, such as rifampicin and cyclosporin A, inhibit OATP1B activity and increase plasma concentrations of OATP1B substrates.[7](#cts12889-bib-0007), [8](#cts12889-bib-0008) Chronic kidney failure has been reported to decrease OATP1B activity, and 3‚Äêcarboxy‚Äê4‚Äêmethyl‚Äê5‚Äêpropyl‚Äê2‚Äêfuranpropanoic acid (CMPF) is suggested as a causative substance.[9](#cts12889-bib-0009), [10](#cts12889-bib-0010), [11](#cts12889-bib-0011) As genetic factors, *SLCO1B1* exhibits two major single nucleotide polymorphisms: A388G and T521C. These polymorphisms form four haplotypes: *OATP1B1*1a* (c.388A‚Äêc.521T), *OATP1B1*1b* (c.388G‚Äêc.521T), *OATP1B1*5* (c.388A‚Äêc.521C), and *OATP1B1*15* (c.388G‚Äêc.521C).[1](#cts12889-bib-0001), [12](#cts12889-bib-0012) Several studies have revealed that *OATP1B1*5* and *OATP1B1*15* are associated with decreased transporting activities of OATP1B1.[12](#cts12889-bib-0012), [13](#cts12889-bib-0013), [14](#cts12889-bib-0014), [15](#cts12889-bib-0015), [16](#cts12889-bib-0016) In the Japanese population, c.521C shows strong linkage disequilibrium with c.388G (*r* ^2^ = 0.0708, D' = 0.9999).[1](#cts12889-bib-0001) Thus, *OATP1B1*15* is an important polymorphism that affects individual OATP1B activity *in vivo*.
    26‚Üí
    27‚ÜíPhenotyping of OATP1B seems to be a useful tool for quantitative assessment of *in vivo* OATP1B activity. Several probes for phenotyping of OATP1B have been reported, such as probe drugs, including HMG‚ÄêCoA reductase inhibitors, and endogenous probes, including total and direct bilirubin, chenodeoxycholate 24‚Äêglucuronide, glycochenodeoxycholate‚Äê3‚Äêsulfate, and coproporphyrins.[17](#cts12889-bib-0017), [18](#cts12889-bib-0018) Especially coproporphyrin‚ÄêI (CP‚ÄêI) and CP‚ÄêIII in plasma are sensitive and specific endogenous probes for phenotyping OATP1B.[19](#cts12889-bib-0019), [20](#cts12889-bib-0020), [21](#cts12889-bib-0021), [22](#cts12889-bib-0022), [23](#cts12889-bib-0023), [24](#cts12889-bib-0024), [25](#cts12889-bib-0025), [26](#cts12889-bib-0026) Rifampicin increases plasma CP‚ÄêI and CP‚ÄêIII concentrations to a greater extent than other endogenous compounds and rosuvastatin.[22](#cts12889-bib-0022) CP‚ÄêI has been reported to be superior to CP‚ÄêIII as an endogenous OATP1B probe, because CP‚ÄêI is a selective substrate for OATP1B1 and OATP1B3, whereas CP‚ÄêIII is also a substrate for OATP2B1.[19](#cts12889-bib-0019), [20](#cts12889-bib-0020) Thus, quantification of plasma CP‚ÄêI concentration is a potentially useful tool for dose individualization of OATP1B substrates and assessment of OATP1B‚Äêmediated drug‚Äêdrug interactions.
    28‚Üí
    29‚ÜíA few previous small‚Äêscale studies suggested that plasma CP‚ÄêI concentration is affected by OATP1B1 polymorphism, but detailed studies are lacking. Shen *et al*.[25](#cts12889-bib-0025) reported a significant decrease in plasma CP‚ÄêI concentration in healthy volunteers with SLCO1B1 c.388AG compared with SLCO1B1 c.388AA, but there were no data on haplotypes of SLCO1B1 T521C. Yee *et al*.[27](#cts12889-bib-0027) measured plasma CP‚ÄêI concentrations in 16 healthy volunteers and found significant difference among 3 groups: SLCO1B1 c.521TT (*n* = 8), c.521TC (*n* = 6), and c.521CC (*n* = 2), but the number of participants were inadequate and data on haplotypes of SLCO1B1 A388G were unknown. The report by Mori *et al*.[28](#cts12889-bib-0028) showed that plasma CP‚ÄêI concentrations increased in Japanese participants with *OATP1B1*15/*15* compared with those with *OATP1B1*1b/*1b* or *OATP1B1*1b/*15*, but only 2 participants with *OATP1B1*15/*15* were included in the study and statistical analysis was not performed. In the present large‚Äêscale study, we measured plasma CP‚ÄêI concentrations in 391 subjects from the Japanese general population, and evaluated the relationship between plasma CP‚ÄêI concentrations and *OATP1B1* polymorphisms to further assess the utility of plasma CP‚ÄêI concentration as an endogenous OATP1B probe.
    30‚Üí
    31‚Üí## METHODS
    32‚Üí
    33‚Üí### Study participants
    34‚Üí
    35‚ÜíWe analyzed the data of participants recruited in the Japan Multi‚ÄêInstitutional Collaborative Cohort (J‚ÄêMICC) Study. The J‚ÄêMICC study recruited subjects from the general Japanese population, and detected and confirmed gene‚Äìenvironment interactions related to lifestyle‚Äêassociated diseases using genetic and clinical data.[29](#cts12889-bib-0029) All participants in the J‚ÄêMICC study gave written informed consent, and the study protocol was approved by the ethics committees at institutions participating in the J‚ÄêMICC study. We analyzed the data of 500 randomly selected subjects who received a health check in Kyoto Prefectural University of Medicine, and selected 391 participants who met the following inclusion criteria: body mass index lower than 30 kg/m^2^, estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) higher than 60 mL/min/1.73 m^2^, total bilirubin lower than 1.5 mg/dL, and alanine aminotransaminase lower than 100 IU/L. The eGFR was calculated by the Chronic Kidney Disease Epidemiology Collaboration (CKD‚ÄêEPI) equation for Japanese.[30](#cts12889-bib-0030) All plasma samples for quantification of CP‚ÄêI and CMPF were stored in deep freeze (‚àí80¬∞C) and light‚Äêresistant condition. This study was approved by the ethics committees of Kyoto Prefectural University of Medicine (approval number: ERB‚ÄêC‚Äê1384) and Meiji Pharmaceutical University (approval number: 3023).
    36‚Üí
    37‚Üí### Genotyping
    38‚Üí
    39‚ÜíThe 14,539 study participants in the J‚ÄêMICC study from 12 areas of Japan were genotyped at RIKEN Center for Integrative Medicine using a Human OmniExpressExome‚Äê8 version 1.2 BeadChip array (Illumina, San Diego, CA, USA).[31](#cts12889-bib-0031) Quality control filtering of samples and single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) have been reported previously.[31](#cts12889-bib-0031) Briefly, for quality control filtering, samples with inconsistent sex information, one of each pair of samples found to be closely related by descent, and samples with ancestry estimated to be outside the Japanese population were excluded. SNPs with a call rate < 0.98 or a Hardy Weinberg equilibrium *P* value < 1 √ó 10^‚àí6^ were also filtered out. Such quality control filtering resulted in the selection of 14,091 subjects and 873,254 SNPs, from whom we selected the above‚Äêmentioned 391 participants and their data. Among the SNPs that passed quality control, we identified rs2306283 (c.A388G, p.N130D, and exon 5) and rs4149056 (c.T521C, p.V174A, and exon 5) from genomewide association study data, and formed four haplotypes: *OATP1B1*1a* (c.388A‚Äêc.521T), *OATP1B1*1b* (c.388G‚Äêc.521T), *OATP1B1*5* (c.388A‚Äêc.521C), and *OATP1B1*15* (c.388G‚Äêc.521C). Finally, the participants were stratified into six polymorphism groups: *OATP1B1*1b/*1b*, *OATP1B1*1a/*1b*, *OATP1B1*1a/*1a*, *OATP1B1*1b/*15*, *OATP1B1*1a/*15*, and *OATP1B1*15/*15* (**Table** [1](#cts12889-tbl-0001)). CP‚Äê1 may also be transported by other OATPs, such as OATP2B1 (encoded by *SLCO2B1*) and OATP1B3 (encoded by *SLCO1B3*), and also by other types of transporters, such as MRP2 (encoded by *ABCC2*).[19](#cts12889-bib-0019), [32](#cts12889-bib-0032) We therefore evaluated the association of plasma CP‚Äê1 concentration with the following SNPs: rs717620 (*ABCC2* variant; c.C‚Äê24T and 5‚Ä≤UTR),[33](#cts12889-bib-0033) rs12422149 (*SLCO2B1* variant; c.G935A, p.R312Q, and exon 7),[34](#cts12889-bib-0034) rs4149117 (*SLCO1B3* variant; c.T334G, p.S112A, and exon 33),[35](#cts12889-bib-0035) and rs7311358 (*SLCO1B3* variant; c.G699A, p.M233I, and exon 6).[35](#cts12889-bib-0035)

    54‚Üí
